data.title: SNB Capital Starts United International at Neutral on Market Potential
data.description:
SNB Capital on Sunday began coverage of United International Holding (SASE:4083) with a neutral rating and price target of 182.7 Saudi riyals, citing its higher recurring return on equity and the attractiveness of the consumer finance market.

The research firm projects a yearly increase in the Saudi-listed digital financial services company's net income to 251 million riyals from 222 million riyals in 2025, led by the performance of its consumer financing product Baseeta. By 2029, SNB expects net income to jump to 459 million riyals.

"The company has emerged as one of the leading players in the [nonbank financial institution] market with a sector leading ROE of +22%," the research firm said. "We expect the company to record a 2024-2029f earnings CAGR of 15.6%, translating into average ROE of 22.5%."

data.title: SocGen Notes Carney Is Elected New Leader of Canada's Liberal Party; Comments on Canadian Dollar
data.description:
Canada's Liberal party picked a leader on Sunday, noted Societe Generale.

Mark Carney, former Bank of Canada and Bank of England governor, replaces Justin Trudeau and will be sworn in as prime minister this week.

Early elections are possible after the Liberals closed the 20 percentage points deficit with the Conservatives in the polls, wrote the bank in a note to clients.

The decline in full-time employment gains in Canada to 19,700 needs to be viewed in light of the increase of 244,000 since last September, but it's the uncertainty of United States trade tariffs on businesses that may tempt the Bank of Canada to lower rates Wedneday by 25bps to 2.75%, the mid-point of the neutral range, stated SocGen.

New 25% U.S. levies on aluminium and steel go live from Wednesday and 'reciprocal tariffs on Canadian lumber and dairy products before that.

The upside risks for outweigh the downside potential in the near term but a return above the 1.4540 area would be required for the pair to line up a return towards the February high of 1.48, added the bank.

data.title: Rocket Companies to buy real estate firm Redfin in $1.75 billion deal
data.description:
Rocket Companies said on Monday it would acquire real estate firm Redfin in an all-stock deal valued at $1.75 billion.

Redfin's shares rose about 70% before the bell.

Founded in 2004, Redfin provides residential real estate brokerage and mortgage origination services along with reports on the U.S. housing market.

The deal is expected to close in the second or third quarter, Detroit-based fintech Rocket said.

Rocket shareholders will own about 95% of the combined company on a fully diluted basis, while Redfin shareholders will the rest on deal completion.

data.description:
U.S. Stock Futures Fall

U.S. stock futures fell, with a new trading week doing little to calm investors' nerves. President Trump over the weekend refused to rule out the U.S. economy entering a recession this year.

Made-in-U.S.A. Lumber Futures Are Coming to Wall Street

Southern yellow pine futures will begin trading later this month as prices split from those of Canadian lumber.

This week marks the 25th anniversary of the Nasdaq peak, but all wasn't lost in the ensuing downturn.

Output increased 2% on month in January as German industry showed tentative signs of improvement, though that could be threatened by U.S. trade tariffs.

Markets Finally Woke Up to Tariff Reality. Is This a Buying Opportunity?

Your correspondent remains torn, but if you believe in the Trump pitch, now might be a time to take a swing.

How European Defense Stocks Became a Hedge-Fund Home Run

Drastic shifts in the global security environment have sparked a sector rally.

Trump Declines to Rule Out Recession

In an interview that aired Sunday, the president demurred when asked if there were reassurances he could provide to businesses seeking more clarity on tariffs.

Tariff Wars Are Often Short. Their Legacies Aren't.

Economists fear that Trump's tariffs, once they take hold, could have unexpected effects long after he leaves office.

U.S. Dominates Arms Trade but Trump's Moves Risk Rattling Buyers

America has benefited from rising European spending, though the president's move to curb support for Ukraine is raising concerns about future purchases.

China Struggles to Shake Off Disinflationary Pressure as Trade Threats Loom

Consumer prices nosedived in February, ending a year of tepid price growth.

data.title: Germany's Greens will not back Merz's debt brake reforms, say parliamentary heads
data.description:
Germany's Greens will not give their much-needed backing to debt reforms proposed by the conservatives and Social Democrats (SPD), the party's parliamentary co-leaders said on Monday.

"We will recommend to the members of the Green parliamentary group that they do not vote in favour of these amendments," said co-leader Katharina Droege at a news conference alongside Britta Hasselmann.

The conservatives and SPD, who are in talks to form a government after an election last month, need the Greens to reach the two-thirds majority needed in parliament to pass their plans to reform the debt brake and create a 500 billion euro fund for infrastructure.

data.title: The Morning Risk Report: Trump Threatens to Hit Russia With Sanctions in Push for Peace Deal
data.description:
The Morning Risk Report: Trump Threatens to Hit Russia With Sanctions in Push for Peace Deal By David Smagalla

Good morning. President Trump said he is "strongly considering" imposing far-reaching sanctions and tariffs on Russia until a peace agreement is reached in the war in Ukraine.

Push against Russia? "Based on the fact that Russia is absolutely 'pounding' Ukraine on the battlefield right now, I am strongly considering large scale Banking Sanctions, Sanctions, and Tariffs on Russia until a Cease Fire and FINAL SETTLEMENT AGREEMENT ON PEACE IS REACHED," Trump wrote on social media on Friday. He added that both Russia and Ukraine needed to get to the negotiating table "before it is too late." The Kremlin didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.

Why now? Russia launched its latest aerial bombardment of Ukrainian cities early Friday with 67 missiles and 194 attack drones, according to the Ukrainian Air Force. Asked if he thought Russian President Vladimir Putin was striking Ukraine in an effort to take advantage of the intelligence-sharing pause, Trump said: "I actually think he's doing what anybody else would do. I think he wants to get it stopped and settled."

How effective? The efficacy of Trump's threat of tariffs is questionable. Russia exported about $2.9 billion worth of goods to the U.S. in 2024, according to Census Bureau data, down from $29.6 billion in 2021, before the war started. Trump could still further target Russia's oil, including some of its biggest oil producers such as Rosneft and Lukoil.

Also: Why Ukraine's Allies Are Divided Over Using $300 Billion in Russian Assets Compliance

U.S. likely to ban Chinese app DeepSeek from government devices.

The White House is weighing measures to restrict Chinese artificial-intelligence upstart DeepSeek , including banning its chatbot from government devices because of national-security concerns, people familiar with the matter said.

U.S. officials are worried about DeepSeek's handling of user data, which the Chinese company says it stores in servers located in China, the people said. Officials also believe DeepSeek hasn't sufficiently explained how it uses the data it collects and who has access to the data, they said.

Crypto executives descend on D.C. for White House summit.

About two dozen crypto executives descended on Washington D.C. on Friday to attend the first-ever White House crypto summit.

Attendees said they expected the biggest focus of the event to be on the strategic bitcoin reserve . President Trump signed an executive order Thursday that officially established a strategic bitcoin reserve and a stockpile for other cryptocurrencies. Some executives also anticipate that the summit will touch on legislation for stablecoins, a type of cryptocurrency pegged to the U.S. dollar. They also expected to discuss banks' interactions with the crypto industry and decentralized finance.

The Forces That Flipped Trump From Crypto Critic to 'Crypto President' Inside SBF's Campaign for a Presidential Pardon From Trump U.S. and European authorities shut down Garantex , a Russian cryptocurrency exchange that they said had processed almost $100 billion in transactions, a significant portion of which was for criminal and terrorist groups.

Two brokerage units of Robinhood Markets have agreed to pay $29.75 million to settle an investigation by the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority into a range of violations, some of which date back more than a decade.

Andrew Pearse, a former Credit Suisse banker involved in Mozambique's "tuna-bond" scandal, was sentenced Thursday to time served by a Brooklyn federal court.

France's former top intelligence official, Bernard Squarcini, was found guilty Friday of illegally taking advantage of his networks to serve private interests, including those of luxury giant LVMH and its chairman, Bernard Arnault.

Prosecutors have unsealed details of how they allege former New York state gubernatorial aide Linda Sun orchestrated a "pay-to-play scheme" that generated a stream of funds and gifts as she did favors for China.

The Trump administration has revoked a sanctions waiver that allowed Iraq to buy gas and electricity from Iran, U.S. officials said this weekend, intensifying an American pressure campaign on Tehran while complicating ties with a key U.S. partner in the region. Risk

Trump declines to rule out recession.

President Trump declined to rule out that the U.S. economy would contract this year and enter a recession, saying that his sweeping economic agenda could cause short-term turbulence that he believed would drive future prosperity.

Asked in a "Sunday Morning Futures" interview on Fox News if he was expecting a recession this year, Trump replied: "I hate to predict things like that. There is a period of transition because what we're doing is very big."

Tariff Wars Are Often Short. Their Legacies Aren't. Finance Executives Seek Stability Amid Erratic Tariff Shifts Tariff Fights Keep the Fed in a Holding Pattern on Rates

China is waging a 'gray zone' campaign to cement power.

From the choppy waters of the South China Sea and Taiwan Strait to the frozen ridges of the Himalayas, China is pursuing a relentless campaign of expansion, operating in the hazy zone between war and peace to extend its power across Asia.

The Wall Street Journal reviewed years of ship-movement data, satellite images, flight-tracking information and other measures of Chinese activity. Taken together, it shows a clear intensification of tactics meant to intimidate rivals and deepen China's control. Here's how it looks.

China Struggles to Shake Off Disinflationary Pressure as Trade Threats Loom How China Is Challenging the West With Its Trillion-Dollar Infrastructure Plan Mark Carney won the leadership of Canada's Liberal Party on Sunday, putting him in line to replace Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and call an election that suddenly seems winnable for the country's center left.

The U.S. continued to generate jobs at a steady pace in February, offering reassurances that the labor market has remained relatively stable since President Trump took office. But there were warning signs that it could weaken .

Elite law firms formed a proud part of the opposition to the first Trump administration. This time around, the industry is fearful of taking on a president who hasn't shied away from punishing his enemies.

President Trump has demonstrated over the past week that he's determined to get adversaries and allies to the negotiating table. The question now is whether his eagerness for the spectacle of negotiations, and forcing different sides into high-profile diplomacy, actually yields results .

An accelerating advance by Russian and North Korean forces is threatening Ukraine's toehold in Kursk , the slice of Russian territory that Ukrainian officials had hoped would give them leverage in any peace talks.

Prosecutions against alleged sea-cable cutters have been elusive since NATO began responding forcefully to suspected sabotage in the Baltic Sea.

California's economic future hinges on solving the state's crippling housing shortage. The charred landscape around Los Angeles presents a major test . What Else Matters As opponents cry chaos and some in his own party grow anxious, President Trump appears not cowed by his critics but emboldened-determined to double down on the disruptions he's set into motion.

American defense companies have increased their dominance of the global arms trade, buoyed by European nations snapping up U.S. jet fighters and missiles.

Elite law firms formed a proud part of the opposition to the first Trump administration. This time around, the industry is fearful of taking on a president who hasn't shied away from punishing his enemies.

After spending more than $50 billion, Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman's sci-fi-inspired dreams for Neom-an arid-mountain ski resort, a floating business district, and the Line, the 106-mile-long pair of Empire State Building-height skyscrapers that is the project's centerpiece-have collided with reality .

The CEO of OpenAI imagines a future where you'll need to constantly demonstrate that you're not a robot. His 'everything app' is the answer-but first, he needs to look deep into your eyes. About Us

Follow us on X at @WSJRisk . Follow Risk & Compliance editor David Smagalla @DSmagalla_DJ and reporters Mengqi Sun @_MengqiSun and Richard Vanderford @VanderfordRich .

You can reach us by replying to any newsletter, or email David at [david.smagalla@wsj.com].

This article is a text version of a Wall Street Journal newsletter published earlier today.

data.title: Deliveroo Avoids Fight With China's Keeta by Exiting Hong Kong — Market Talk
data.description: Deliveroo has best served the interests of its shareholders by exiting Hong Kong and avoiding a fightback against Keeta, Jefferies analysts say. Keeta, the international arm of China's biggest food delivery app Meituan, entered that market in May 2023. In mid-January, U.K.'s Deliveroo said it was seeing a decline in its gross transaction value--a closely watched industry metric--in Hong Kong, the analysts note. With Deliveroo's exit, Hong Kong once again becomes a duopoly, but they don't expect Deliveroo's exit nor the sale of customer relationships to rival foodpanda, owned by Delivery Hero, to be blocked by antitrust authorities, the analysts say. Shares in Deliveroo are up 1.7% at 1.27 pounds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

data.title: India's Godrej Consumer mulls more gradual price hikes for soaps
data.description:
India's Godrej Consumer Products will keep raising prices of its soaps gradually to protect margins amid rising palm oil prices, the consumer goods maker's top boss said on Monday.

Palm oil prices have surged in recent months due to floods in top producers Indonesia and Malaysia, forcing consumer goods makers, including Dove soapmaker Hindustan Unilever and Cinthol owner Godrej Consumer, to raise prices.

"We have not recovered the full extent of the costs yet," Godrej Consumer CEO Sudhir Sitapati told Reuters in the southern Indian state of Tamil Nadu.

It would take 2-to-3 quarters to widen margins, but the company will not push up prices suddenly, the CEO said.

Sitapati does not expect the price hikes to have an impact on sales as palm oil-based products, including soap, tend "not to be discretionary" goods that consumers can forgo.

Soaps make up about a fifth of Godrej Consumer's revenue.

Middle-class Indians, particularly city dwellers, have been cutting spending on everything from cookies to fast food due to elevated inflation and slowing economic growth.

The impact of palm oil prices on margins of larger rival Hindustan Unilever, which has been reformulating its soaps to cut the use of palm oil, is lower, analysts have said.

Godrej Consumer's CEO ruled out reformulating soaps to reduce the use of palm oil.

The company's gross margin narrowed 175 basis points during the October-to-December period from a year earlier, the first shrinkage in two years, as prices of palm oil surged.

data.title: Pakistan central bank surprises with hold on key policy rate
data.description:
Pakistan's central bank held its key policy rate at 12% on Monday, it said in a statement, a widely unexpected move which halted an easing cycle that witnessed six straight reductions since June.

The central bank has slashed rates by 1000 bps from an all time high of 22% in June 2024, to revive economic sentiment and growth, while navigating reforms under a $7 billion facility from the International Monetary Fund (IMF) in September.

"On balance, the MPC (monetary policy committee) assessed the current real interest rate to be adequately positive on forward-looking basis to sustain the ongoing macroeconomic stability," the bank statement said.

The State Bank of Pakistan (SBP), despite the halt in cuts, is one of the most aggressive central banks among central banks of emerging markets during the current easing cycle and has topped the 625 bps in rate cuts it did in 2020 during the COVID-19 pandemic.

At its last policy meeting, SBP kept its forecast of full-year GDP growth at 2.5% to 3.5%, and predicted faster growth would help boost foreign exchange reserves that had been lacklustre.

Pakistan's economy grew by 0.92% in the first quarter of the fiscal year 2024-25 which ends in June.

Ten of 14 analysts surveyed by Reuters expected the central bank to cut its key rate, while four expected it to hold the rate. Analysts surveyed said they expect inflation may pick up in May as the base year effect wears off.

Pakistan's consumer inflation rate slowed to a near decade low of 1.5% in February, largely due to a high year-ago base. That was below the government's forecast and significantly lower than a multi-decade high of around 40% in May 2023.

data.title: British Pound Hovers at 4-Month Highs
data.description:
The British pound traded around $1.29, hovering near four-month highs, supported by broad dollar weakness amid concerns over the US economy and the potential impact of upcoming tariffs.

Sterling is also gaining strength from expectations that UK interest rates will remain elevated for longer, with traders scaling back bets on Bank of England rate cuts to 52bps for 2025.

Looking ahead, investors will closely watch monthly GDP data this week for insights into the UK’s economic performance in January.

Additionally, on March 26, the Office for Budget Responsibility, the UK’s public finance watchdog, will release its latest forecasts on the economy and borrowing.

data.description: Coterra Energy has an average rating of buy and mean price target of $35.18, according to analysts polled by FactSet.

data.title: Canola Futures Plunge to 3-Month Low
data.description:
Canola futures dropped over 7% to near CAD 580 per tonne, their lowest level since early December 2024, following sudden China's retaliatory tariffs that take aim at canola, pork and other food commodities.

China is hitting Canada with 100% tariffs on canola oil and peas, and 25% tariffs on pork and aquatic products in retaliation for Canada's 100% tariff on Chinese-made electric vehicles and heavy levies on steel and aluminum imports.

The tariffs will likely lead to a sharp decline in Canadian exports of these products to China, which has been a major market for Canadian rapeseed producers.
